access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2
notes: The current catalog explicitly reports the digital images as unavailable.

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Pearl and of Cleanness, Patience, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. Pearl occupies modern folios 41r–59v; the older foliation embedded in the repository XML runs 39r–55v.

historical_designations: Cotton MS Nero A X, article 3
Cotton MS Nero A X, ff. 41–130
holding.notes: The original composite Cotton MS Nero A X was separated into two volumes in 1964.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2
notes: Do not describe the repository XML's folio milestones as modern BL foliation.
The witness date is separate from the poem's composition date and the later illustration date.

physicalDescription.extent: 90 folios in the separately bound volume; Pearl at modern folios 41r–59v
physicalDescription.format: Parchment codex, written in one column of 36 lines
notes: Gothic cursive script described as textura rotunda with anglicana features.
The manuscript has two foliation sequences; the BL catalog uses modern pencil foliation, while the XML milestones preserve the older sequence.
The text was copied by one scribe in a South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire dialect; the poet's dialect is distinguished as farther south in Staffordshire.
Four full-page Pearl drawings at modern folios 41r–42v precede the written poem; the BL catalog describes the dreamer, Pearl Maiden, river, and heavenly Jerusalem scenes.

rights.basis: The text is attested in a manuscript copied between 1375 and 1400 and is centuries beyond any U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval textual content of Pearl
notes: This concerns the old text, not modern photographs, scans, transcriptions, editions, catalog prose, or provider contracts.

lineage.summary: The checked-in Pearl XML is a local repository representation of a University of Michigan electronic text whose legacy header names Oxford Text Archive U-1686-A and a 1993 distribution date. The current OTA 1686 record is separately dated 1992-04-03. Their declared immediate source is E. V. Gordon's 1953 Clarendon Press edition, finalized for publication after his death by Ida L. Gordon; that edition represents the unique medieval witness, British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2. The manuscript, scholarly edition, electronic versions, and repository artifact are modeled separately so that composition, copying, publication, access, foliation, and rights statements remain scoped to the correct layer.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous (Pearl Poet)
catalogSummary.region: Staffordshire, England (poet's dialect); unique witness copied in the North-West Midlands
catalogSummary.title: Pearl

catalogingSubject.label: Pearl
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English poem conventionally titled Pearl, not any particular manuscript, scholarly edition, digital encoding, translation, or modern adaptation.

contentParts.label: Section I
contentParts.summary: The narrator mourns his lost pearl, falls asleep in the garden where it was lost, and begins the dream vision.

contentParts.label: Section II
contentParts.summary: The dreamer explores a radiant landscape, follows a stream, and sees a richly adorned maiden on the opposite bank.

contentParts.label: Section III
contentParts.summary: He recognizes the Pearl Maiden; she answers his grief and rebukes his mistaken assumptions about death and heavenly life.

contentParts.label: Section IV
contentParts.summary: The dreamer asks about her queenship; she distinguishes the Virgin Mary's unique rank from the blessed souls who are brides of the Lamb.

contentParts.label: Section V
contentParts.summary: He objects that she died too young to earn such reward; she begins the parable of the workers in the vineyard to explain divine grace.

contentParts.label: Section VI
contentParts.summary: The vineyard parable continues as workers hired at different hours receive the same penny.

contentParts.label: Section VII
contentParts.summary: The maiden answers objections to equal reward: salvation is God's free gift, not a wage proportioned by human calculation.

contentParts.label: Section VIII
contentParts.summary: She explains the salvation of innocent children through Christ's grace and teaching about receiving the kingdom as a child.

contentParts.label: Section IX
contentParts.summary: The maiden identifies herself among the spotless company of the Lamb and develops pearl imagery for purity, value, and heaven's gate.

contentParts.label: Section X
contentParts.summary: The dreamer asks to cross the separating water; she tells him that no living person may pass into her country before death.

contentParts.label: Section XI
contentParts.summary: She names her dwelling as the New Jerusalem; the dreamer asks how it relates to the historical earthly city.

contentParts.label: Section XII
contentParts.summary: The maiden distinguishes the two Jerusalems and recounts Christ's sacrificial death as the spotless Lamb.

contentParts.label: Section XIII
contentParts.summary: She describes the heavenly city illuminated by God and the Lamb; the dreamer asks to be shown it.

contentParts.label: Section XIV
contentParts.summary: Permitted to see but not enter the city, the dreamer proceeds along the river toward the vision.

contentParts.label: Section XV
contentParts.summary: In the poem's six-stanza central expansion, he sees the New Jerusalem's jeweled walls, foundations, gates, and crystalline brilliance.

contentParts.label: Section XVI
contentParts.summary: The vision continues with the city's ordered dimensions, divine light, purity, and blessed inhabitants.

contentParts.label: Section XVII
contentParts.summary: The Lamb appears in procession with the company of virgins amid music, light, and concord.

contentParts.label: Section XVIII
contentParts.summary: The dreamer beholds the 144,000 in pearl-white array and searches the harmonious procession for the maiden.

contentParts.label: Section XIX
contentParts.summary: He finds his Pearl among the blessed company, and longing to join her overwhelms the limits placed upon his vision.

contentParts.label: Section XX
contentParts.summary: He tries to cross the river, is expelled from the dream, awakens in the garden, and closes by commending the pearl to God and accepting divine will.

assertion.notes: This is a conservative work-level dating distinct from the legacy XML profile date 1400 and the catalogue dates assigned to copying and decoration.

notes: The copying date is an attestation range and does not establish an exact composition year.

extent.description: The repository XML contains twenty sections, 101 LG stanza elements, and 1,212 L elements. Nineteen sections contain five twelve-line stanzas; Section XV contains six.

formStatements.description: One hundred and one twelve-line rhymed stanzas, richly alliterative and linked by repeated concatenation words, arranged by the source edition into twenty sections.

assertion.notes: The elegiac reading is secure at the level of represented grief and consolation; it does not establish that the narrator is straightforward autobiography.

notes: The legacy XML profile date 1400 is not treated as an exact composition date.
The catalog distinguishes the poem, the sole manuscript witness, Gordon's 1953 source edition, the U-M/OTA digital encoding, and the checked-in repository artifact.
The XML's older foliation and the BL catalog's modern foliation are both recorded in lineage rather than silently reconciled.

next_steps: Review current scholarship on elegy, allegory, and autobiographical inference.
Do not convert the narrator's represented relationship into author biography without independent evidence.
openQuestions.question: Does the Pearl Maiden represent the poet's own deceased daughter, or should the first-person loss remain cataloged only as the poem's represented situation?

next_steps: Review current dating and authorship studies while separating scribal unity from authorial identity.
Preserve competing conclusions as separately evidenced assertions.
openQuestions.question: Can Pearl's date and common authorship with the other Cotton Nero A X/2 poems be stated more precisely than the conservative late-fourteenth-century anonymous attribution?

assertion.notes: The BL catalog distinguishes the poet's dialect, thought to be farther south in Staffordshire, from the surviving copy's scribal dialect.

notes: Dialect localization is not proof of an exact place of composition or the author's residence.
This statement concerns the unique manuscript's scribe, not necessarily the author's original spelling system.
placeStatements.placeLabel: South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire, England

assertion.notes: Pearl Poet is a conventional label for an unknown author, not an attested personal name.

summaries.value: A grieving dreamer falls asleep in a garden where his lost pearl was buried and enters a jeweled visionary landscape. Across a river he meets the Pearl Maiden, who corrects his earthly assumptions about loss, merit, innocence, grace, and heavenly reward. She reveals the New Jerusalem and joins the Lamb's procession. When the dreamer tries to cross the river, he awakens and ends by submitting his pearl and himself to God's will.

Pearl survives uniquely in British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2 with Cleanness, Patience, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. The repository text descends through E. V. Gordon's 1953 scholarly edition and a U-M/OTA electronic encoding that omits the edition's preface, contents, abbreviations, introduction, bibliography, notes, and appendixes.

assertion.notes: Confirmed as the established preferred title; the word derives from the poem's recurrent image and incipit rather than a manuscript title heading.

work.author: Anonymous (Pearl Poet)
work.regionDisplay: Staffordshire, England (poet's dialect); unique witness copied in the North-West Midlands

regions.label: South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire, England

work.summary: A grieving dreamer falls asleep in a garden where his lost pearl was buried and enters a jeweled visionary landscape. Across a river he meets the Pearl Maiden, who corrects his earthly assumptions about loss, merit, innocence, grace, and heavenly reward. She reveals the New Jerusalem and joins the Lamb's procession. When the dreamer tries to cross the river, he awakens and ends by submitting his pearl and himself to God's will.
